title: Energy Policy Act of 2005

An Act to ensure jobs for our future with secure, affordable, and reliable energy.
the [109th United States Congress](/wiki/109th_United_States_Congress "109th United States Congress")

[42 U.S.C.](/wiki/Title_42_of_the_United_States_Code "Title 42 of the United States Code") [ch. 149](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/42/chapter-149) § 15801 et seq.

[16 U.S.C.](/wiki/Title_16_of_the_United_States_Code "Title 16 of the United States Code") [ch. 46](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/16/chapter-46) § 2601 et seq. [42 U.S.C.](/wiki/Title_42_of_the_United_States_Code "Title 42 of the United States Code") [ch. 134](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/42/chapter-134) § 13201 et seq.

in the House as [H.R. 6](https://www.congress.gov/bill/109th-congress/house-bill/6) by [Joe Barton](/wiki/Joe_Barton "Joe Barton") ([R](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)")–[TX](/wiki/Texas "Texas")) on April 18, 2005 * **Committee consideration** by [House Energy and Commerce](/wiki/United_States_House_Committee_on_Energy_and_Commerce "United States House Committee on Energy and Commerce"), [House Education and the Workforce](/wiki/United_States_House_Committee_on_Education_and_the_Workforce "United States House Committee on Education and the Workforce"), [House Financial Services](/wiki/United_States_House_Committee_on_Financial_Services "United States House Committee on Financial Services"), [House Agriculture](/wiki/United_States_House_Committee_on_Agriculture "United States House Committee on Agriculture"), [House Resources](/wiki/United_States_House_Committee_on_Natural_Resources "United States House Committee on Natural Resources"), [House Science](/wiki/United_States_House_Committee_on_Science,_Space_and_Technology "United States House Committee on Science, Space and Technology"), [House Ways and Means](/wiki/United_States_House_Committee_on_Ways_and_Means "United States House Committee on Ways and Means"), [House Transportation and Infrastructure](/wiki/United_States_House_Committee_on_Transportation_and_Infrastructure "United States House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure") * **Passed the House** on April 21, 2005 (249-183, [Roll call vote 132](https://clerk.house.gov/evs/2005/roll132.xml), via Clerk.House.gov) * **Passed the Senate** on June 28, 2005 (85-12, [Roll call vote 158](http://www.senate.gov/legislative/LIS/roll_call_lists/roll_call_vote_cfm.cfm?congress=109&session=1&vote=00158), via Senate.gov) * **Reported by the joint conference committee** on July 27, 2005; **agreed to by the House** on July 28, 2005 (275-156, [Roll call vote 445](https://clerk.house.gov/evs/2005/roll445.xml), via Clerk.House.gov) and by the **Senate** on July 29, 2005 (74-26, [Roll call vote 213](http://www.senate.gov/legislative/LIS/roll_call_lists/roll_call_vote_cfm.cfm?congress=109&session=1&vote=00213), via Senate.gov) * **Signed into law** by President [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") on August 8, 2005 | |

([Pub. L.](/wiki/Act_of_Congress#Public_law,_private_law,_designation "Act of Congress") [109–58 (text)](https://www.govinfo.gov/link/plaw/109/public/58?link-type=html) [(PDF)](https://www.govinfo.gov/link/plaw/109/public/58?link-type=pdf&.pdf)) is a [federal law](/wiki/Federal_law "Federal law") signed by President [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") on August 8, 2005, at [Sandia National Laboratories](/wiki/Sandia_National_Laboratories "Sandia National Laboratories") in [Albuquerque, New Mexico](/wiki/Albuquerque,_New_Mexico "Albuquerque, New Mexico"). The act, described by proponents as an attempt to combat growing energy problems, changed [US energy policy](/wiki/US_energy_policy "US energy policy") by providing [tax incentives](/wiki/Energy_subsidies#Allocation_of_subsidies_in_the_United_States "Energy subsidies") and loan guarantees for energy production of various types. The most consequential aspect of the law was to greatly increase ethanol production[[2]](#cite_note-2) to be blended with gasoline. The law also repealed the [Public Utility Holding Company Act of 1935](/wiki/Public_Utility_Holding_Company_Act_of_1935 "Public Utility Holding Company Act of 1935"), effective February 2006.[[3]](#cite_note-repeal-3)

[George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") signing the Energy Policy Act of 2005, which was designed to promote US nuclear reactor construction, through incentives and subsidies, including cost-overrun support up to a total of $2 billion for six new nuclear plants.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The **Energy Policy Act of 2005** ([Pub. L.](/wiki/Act_of_Congress#Public_law,_private_law,_designation "Act of Congress") [109–58 (text)](https://www.govinfo.gov/link/plaw/109/public/58?link-type=html) [(PDF)](https://www.govinfo.gov/link/plaw/109/public/58?link-type=pdf&.pdf)) is a [federal law](/wiki/Federal_law "Federal law") signed by President [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") on August 8, 2005, at [Sandia National Laboratories](/wiki/Sandia_National_Laboratories "Sandia National Laboratories") in [Albuquerque, New Mexico](/wiki/Albuquerque,_New_Mexico "Albuquerque, New Mexico"). The act, described by proponents as an attempt to combat growing energy problems, changed [US energy policy](/wiki/US_energy_policy "US energy policy") by providing [tax incentives](/wiki/Energy_subsidies#Allocation_of_subsidies_in_the_United_States "Energy subsidies") and loan guarantees for energy production of various types. The most consequential aspect of the law was to greatly increase ethanol production[[2]](#cite_note-2) to be blended with gasoline. The law also repealed the [Public Utility Holding Company Act of 1935](/wiki/Public_Utility_Holding_Company_Act_of_1935 "Public Utility Holding Company Act of 1935"), effective February 2006.[[3]](#cite_note-repeal-3)

## Provisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Energy_Policy_Act_of_2005&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Provisions")]

### General provisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Energy_Policy_Act_of_2005&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: General provisions")]

* The Act increases the amount of [biofuel](/wiki/Biofuel "Biofuel") (usually [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol")) that must be mixed with gasoline sold in the United States to 4 billion US gallons (15,000,000 m3) by 2006, 6.1 billion US gallons (23,000,000 m3) by 2009 and 7.5 billion US gallons (28,000,000 m3) by 2012;[[4]](#cite_note-4) two years later, the [Energy Independence and Security Act of 2007](/wiki/Energy_Independence_and_Security_Act_of_2007 "Energy Independence and Security Act of 2007") extended the target to 36 billion US gallons (140,000,000 m3) by 2022.[[5]](#cite_note-5)
* Under an amendment in the [American Recovery and Reinvestment Act of 2009](/wiki/American_Recovery_and_Reinvestment_Act_of_2009 "American Recovery and Reinvestment Act of 2009"), Section 406, the Energy Policy Act of 2005 authorizes [loan guarantees](/wiki/Loan_guarantee "Loan guarantee") for innovative technologies that avoid [greenhouse gases](/wiki/Greenhouse_gases "Greenhouse gases"), which might include advanced [nuclear reactor](/wiki/Nuclear_reactor "Nuclear reactor") designs, such as [pebble bed modular reactors](/wiki/Pebble_bed_modular_reactor "Pebble bed modular reactor") (PBMRs) as well as [carbon capture and storage](/wiki/Carbon_capture_and_storage "Carbon capture and storage") and [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy");
* It seeks to increase [coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal") as an energy source while also reducing air pollution, through authorizing $200 million annually for clean coal initiatives, repealing the current 160-acre (0.65 km2) cap on coal leases, allowing the advanced payment of royalties from coal mines and requiring an assessment of coal resources on federal lands that are not national parks;
* It authorizes tax credits for [wind](/wiki/Wind_energy "Wind energy") and other [alternative energy](/wiki/Alternative_energy "Alternative energy") producers;
* It adds ocean energy sources, including [wave](/wiki/Wave_power "Wave power") and [tidal power](/wiki/Tidal_power "Tidal power") for the first time as separately identified, renewable technologies;
* It authorizes $50 million annually over the life of the law for biomass grants;
* It includes provisions aimed at making [geothermal energy](/wiki/Geothermal_energy "Geothermal energy") more competitive with fossil fuels in generating electricity;
* It requires the [Department of Energy](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Energy "United States Department of Energy") to:
  + Study and report on existing natural energy resources including wind, solar, waves and tides;
  + Study and report on national benefits of [demand response](/wiki/Demand_response "Demand response") and make a recommendation on achieving specific levels of benefits and encourages [time-based pricing](/wiki/Time-based_pricing "Time-based pricing") and other forms of demand response as a policy decision;
  + Designate [National Interest Electric Transmission Corridors](/wiki/National_Interest_Electric_Transmission_Corridor "National Interest Electric Transmission Corridor") where there are significant [transmission](/wiki/Electric_power_transmission "Electric power transmission") limitations adversely affecting the public (the [Federal Energy Regulatory Commission](/wiki/Federal_Energy_Regulatory_Commission "Federal Energy Regulatory Commission") may authorize federal permits for transmission projects in these regions);
  + Report in one year on how to dispose of [high-level nuclear waste](/wiki/High_level_waste "High level waste");
* It authorizes the [Department of the Interior](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Interior "United States Department of the Interior") to grant leases for activity that involves the production, transportation or transmission of energy on the [Outer Continental Shelf](/wiki/Outer_Continental_Shelf "Outer Continental Shelf") lands from sources other than gas and oil (Section 388);[[6]](#cite_note-6)
* It requires all [public](/wiki/Public_utility "Public utility") [electric utilities](/wiki/Electric_utility "Electric utility") to offer [net metering](/wiki/Net_metering "Net metering") on request to their customers;
* It prohibits the manufacture and importation of [mercury-vapor lamp](/wiki/Mercury-vapor_lamp "Mercury-vapor lamp") ballasts after January 1, 2008;
* It provides [tax breaks](/wiki/Tax_break "Tax break") for those making [energy conservation](/wiki/Energy_conservation "Energy conservation") improvements to their homes;
* It provides incentives to companies to [drill for oil](/wiki/Offshore_drilling "Offshore drilling") in the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico");
* It exempts oil and gas producers from certain requirements of the [Safe Drinking Water Act](/wiki/Safe_Drinking_Water_Act "Safe Drinking Water Act");
* It extends the [daylight saving time](/wiki/Daylight_saving_time "Daylight saving time") by four to five weeks, depending upon the year (see below);
* It requires that no drilling for gas or oil may be done in or underneath the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes");
* It requires that the Federal Fleet vehicles capable of operating on alternative fuels be operated on these fuels exclusively (Section 701);
* It sets federal reliability standards regulating the electrical grid (done in response to the [2003 North America blackout](/wiki/2003_North_America_blackout "2003 North America blackout"));[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)
* It includes nuclear-specific provisions;[[10]](#cite_note-10)
  + It extends the [Price-Anderson Nuclear Industries Indemnity Act](/wiki/Price-Anderson_Nuclear_Industries_Indemnity_Act "Price-Anderson Nuclear Industries Indemnity Act") through 2025;
  + It authorizes cost-overrun support of up to $2 billion total for up to six new [nuclear power plants](/wiki/Nuclear_power_plant "Nuclear power plant");
  + It authorizes production tax credit of up to $125 million total a year, estimated at 1.8 US¢/kWh during the first eight years of operation for the first 6.000 MW of capacity,[[11]](#cite_note-11) consistent with renewables;
  + It authorizes loan guarantees of up to 80% of project cost to be repaid within 30 years or 90% of the project's life;[[12]](#cite_note-12)
  + It authorizes $2.95 billion for R&D and the building of an [advanced hydrogen cogeneration reactor](/wiki/Next_Generation_Nuclear_Plant "Next Generation Nuclear Plant") at [Idaho National Laboratory](/wiki/Idaho_National_Laboratory "Idaho National Laboratory");[[13]](#cite_note-13)
  + It authorizes 'standby support' for new reactor delays that offset the financial impact of delays beyond the industry's control for the first six reactors, including 100% coverage of the first two plants with up to $500 million each and 50% of the cost of delays for plants three through six with up to $350 million each for;[[14]](#cite_note-14)
  + It allows nuclear plant employees and certain contractors to carry firearms;
  + It prohibits the sale, export or transfer of nuclear materials and "sensitive nuclear technology" to any state sponsor of terrorist activities;
  + It updates tax treatment of decommissioning funds;
* The law exempted fluids used in the [natural gas extraction](/wiki/Natural_gas_extraction "Natural gas extraction") process of [hydraulic fracturing](/wiki/Hydraulic_fracturing "Hydraulic fracturing") (fracking) from protections under the [Clean Air Act](/wiki/Clean_Air_Act_(United_States) "Clean Air Act (United States)"), [Clean Water Act](/wiki/Clean_Water_Act "Clean Water Act"), [Safe Drinking Water Act](/wiki/Safe_Drinking_Water_Act "Safe Drinking Water Act"), and [CERCLA](/wiki/CERCLA "CERCLA") ("Superfund").[[15]](#cite_note-Kosnik-15)
* It directs the Secretary of the Interior to complete a programmatic [environmental impact statement](/wiki/Environmental_impact_statement "Environmental impact statement") for a commercial leasing program for [oil shale](/wiki/Oil_shale "Oil shale") and [tar sands](/wiki/Tar_sands "Tar sands") resources on public lands with an emphasis on the most geologically prospective lands within each of the states of [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado"), [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah"), and [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming").[[16]](#cite_note-anl-16)

### Tax reductions by subject area

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Energy_Policy_Act_of_2005&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Tax reductions by subject area")]

* $4.3 billion for [nuclear power](/wiki/Nuclear_power "Nuclear power")[[17]](#cite_note-17)
* $2.8 billion for [fossil fuel](/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel") production
* $2.7 billion to extend the [renewable electricity production](/wiki/Renewable_electricity_production "Renewable electricity production") credit
* $1.6 billion in tax incentives for investments in "[clean coal](/wiki/Clean_coal "Clean coal")" facilities
* $1.3 billion for [energy conservation](/wiki/Energy_conservation "Energy conservation") and [efficiency](/wiki/Efficient_energy_use "Efficient energy use")
* $1.3 billion for [alternative fuel vehicles](/wiki/Alternative_fuel_vehicle "Alternative fuel vehicle") and [fuels](/wiki/Alternative_fuel "Alternative fuel") ([bioethanol](/wiki/Bioethanol "Bioethanol"), [biomethane](/wiki/Biomethane "Biomethane"), [liquified natural gas](/wiki/Liquified_natural_gas "Liquified natural gas"), [propane](/wiki/Propane "Propane"))
* $500 million [Clean Renewable Energy Bonds](/wiki/Clean_Renewable_Energy_Bonds "Clean Renewable Energy Bonds") (CREBS) for [government agencies](/wiki/Government_agency "Government agency") for renewable energy projects.

### Change to daylight saving time

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Energy_Policy_Act_of_2005&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Change to daylight saving time")]

Further information: [Daylight saving time in the United States](/wiki/Daylight_saving_time_in_the_United_States "Daylight saving time in the United States")

The law amended the [Uniform Time Act](/wiki/Uniform_Time_Act "Uniform Time Act") of 1966 by changing the start and end dates of [daylight saving time](/wiki/Daylight_saving_time "Daylight saving time"), beginning in 2007. Clocks were set ahead one hour on the second Sunday of March (March 11, 2007) instead of on the first Sunday of April (April 1, 2007). Clocks were set back one hour on the first Sunday of November (November 4, 2007), rather than on the last Sunday of October (October 28, 2007). This had the net effect of slightly lengthening the duration of daylight saving time.

Lobbyists for this provision included the Sporting Goods Manufacturers Association, the [National Association of Convenience Stores](/wiki/National_Association_of_Convenience_Stores "National Association of Convenience Stores"), and the National [Retinitis Pigmentosa](/wiki/Retinitis_Pigmentosa "Retinitis Pigmentosa") Foundation Fighting Blindness.

Lobbyists against this provision included the [U.S. Conference of Catholic Bishops](/wiki/U.S._Conference_of_Catholic_Bishops "U.S. Conference of Catholic Bishops"), the [United Synagogue of Conservative Judaism](/wiki/United_Synagogue_of_Conservative_Judaism "United Synagogue of Conservative Judaism"), the National [Parent-Teacher Association](/wiki/Parent-Teacher_Association "Parent-Teacher Association"), the Calendaring and Scheduling Consortium, the [Edison Electric Institute](/wiki/Edison_Electric_Institute "Edison Electric Institute"), and the [Air Transport Association](/wiki/Air_Transport_Association "Air Transport Association").[[18]](#cite_note-18) This section of the act is controversial; some have questioned whether daylight saving results in net energy savings.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Commercial building deduction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Energy_Policy_Act_of_2005&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Commercial building deduction")]

The Act created the Energy Efficient [Commercial Buildings Tax Deduction](/w/index.php?title=Commercial_Buildings_Tax_Deduction&action=edit&redlink=1 "Commercial Buildings Tax Deduction (page does not exist)"), a special financial incentive designed to reduce the initial cost of investing in [energy-efficient building](/wiki/Green_building "Green building") systems via an accelerated tax deduction under section §179D of the Internal Revenue Code (IRC)[[1]](https://web.archive.org/web/20120628180426/http://www.capitalreviewgroup.com/energy-certification/179d-tax-deduction/) Many building owners are unaware that the [Policy Act of 2005] includes a tax deduction (§179D) for investments in "energy efficient commercial building property" designed to significantly reduce the heating, cooling, water heating and interior lighting cost of new or existing commercial buildings placed into service between January 1, 2006, and December 31, 2013.
§179D includes full and partial tax deductions for investments in energy efficient commercial building that are designed to increase the efficiency of energy-consuming functions. Up to $.60 for lighting$, .60 for HVAC and $.60 for building envelope, creating a potential deduction of $1.80 per sq/ft. Interior lighting may also be improved using the Interim Lighting Rule, which provides a simplified process to earn the Deduction, capped at $0.30-$0.60/square foot. Improvements are compared to a baseline of [ASHRAE](/wiki/American_Society_of_Heating,_Refrigerating_and_Air-Conditioning_Engineers "American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers") 2001 standards.[[20]](#cite_note-cbd2-20)

To obtain these benefits the facilities/energy division of a business, its tax department, and a firm specializing in EPAct 179D deductions needed to cooperate. IRS mandated software had to be used and an independent 3rd party had to certify the qualification. For municipal buildings, benefits were passed through to the primary designers/architects in an attempt to encourage innovative municipal design.

The Commercial Buildings Tax Deduction expiration date had been extended twice, last by the [Energy Improvement and Extension Act of 2008](/wiki/Energy_Improvement_and_Extension_Act_of_2008 "Energy Improvement and Extension Act of 2008"). With this extension, the CBTD could be claimed for qualifying projects completed before January 1, 2014.[[20]](#cite_note-cbd2-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Energy management

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Energy_Policy_Act_of_2005&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Energy management")]

The commercial building tax deductions[[22]](#cite_note-22) could be used to improve the payback period of a prospective energy improvement investment. The deductions could be combined by participating in demand response programs where building owners agree to curtail usage at peak times for a premium. The most common qualifying projects were in the area of lighting.

## Cost estimate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Energy_Policy_Act_of_2005&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Cost estimate")]

The [Congressional Budget Office](/wiki/Congressional_Budget_Office "Congressional Budget Office") (CBO) review of the conference version of the bill estimated the Act would increase direct spending by $2.2 billion over the 2006–2010 period, and by $1.6 billion over the 2006–2015 period. The CBO did not attempt to estimate additional effects on discretionary spending. The CBO and the Joint Committee on Taxation estimated that the legislation would reduce revenues by $7.9 billion over the 2005–2010 period and by $12.3 billion over the 2005–2015 period.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The collective reduction in national consumption of energy (gas and electricity) is significant for home heating. The Act provided gible financial incentives (tax credits) for average homeowners to make environmentally positive changes to their homes. It made improvements to home energy use more affordable for walls, doors, windows, roofs, water heaters, etc. Consumer spending, and hence the national economy, was abetted. Industry grew for manufacture of these environmentally positive improvements. These positive improvements have been near and long-term in effect.

The collective reduction in national consumption of oil is significant for automotive vehicles. The Act provided tangible financial incentives (tax credits) for operators of hybrid vehicles. It helped fuel competition among auto makers to meet rising demands for [fuel-efficient vehicles](/wiki/Fuel_economy_in_automobiles "Fuel economy in automobiles"). Consumer spending, and hence the national economy, was abetted. Dependence on imported oil was reduced. The national trade deficit was improved. Industry grew for manufacture of these environmentally positive improvements. These positive improvements have been near and long-term in effect.

## Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Energy_Policy_Act_of_2005&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Criticism")]

* The *[Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")* contended that the spending bill was a broad collection of subsidies for United States energy companies; in particular, the nuclear and oil industries.[[24]](#cite_note-24)
* Speaking for the National Republicans for Environmental Protection Association, President Martha Marks said that the organization was disappointed in the law because it did not support conservation enough, and continued to subsidize the well-established oil and gas industries that didn't require subsidizing.[[25]](#cite_note-25)
* The law did not include provisions for drilling in the [Arctic National Wildlife Refuge](/wiki/Arctic_National_Wildlife_Refuge "Arctic National Wildlife Refuge") (ANWR); some Republicans claimed "access to the abundant [oil reserves](/wiki/Oil_reserves "Oil reserves") in ANWR would strengthen America's energy independence without harming the environment."[[26]](#cite_note-26)
* Senator [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton") criticized Senator [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama")'s vote for the bill in the [2008 Democratic Primary](/wiki/2008_Democratic_Primary "2008 Democratic Primary").[[27]](#cite_note-27)

## Legislative history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Energy_Policy_Act_of_2005&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Legislative history")]

The Act was voted on and passed twice by the [United States Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate"), once prior to [conference committee](/wiki/Conference_committee "Conference committee"), and once after. In both cases, there were numerous senators who voted against the [bill](/wiki/Bill_(proposed_law) "Bill (proposed law)"). [John McCain](/wiki/John_McCain "John McCain"), the [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") nominee for [President of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") in the [2008 election](/wiki/2008_United_States_presidential_election "2008 United States presidential election") voted against the bill. Democrat [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"), President of the United States from January 2009 to January 2017, voted in favor of the bill.

### Provisions in the original bill that were not in the act

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Energy_Policy_Act_of_2005&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Provisions in the original bill that were not in the act")]

* Limited liability for producers of [MTBE](/wiki/MTBE "MTBE").
* Drilling for oil in the [Arctic National Wildlife Refuge](/wiki/Arctic_National_Wildlife_Refuge "Arctic National Wildlife Refuge") (ANWR).
* Increasing vehicle efficiency standards ([CAFE](/wiki/Corporate_Average_Fuel_Economy "Corporate Average Fuel Economy")).
* Requiring increased reliance on non-greenhouse gas-emitting energy sources similar to the [Kyoto Protocol](/wiki/Kyoto_Protocol "Kyoto Protocol").

To remove from 18 CFR Part 366.1 the definitions of "electric utility company" and [exempt wholesale generator](/wiki/Power_purchase_agreement "Power purchase agreement") (EWG), that an EWG is not an electric utility company.[[3]](#cite_note-repeal-3)
